template:
**DOCUMENT FORMAT — POLICY LIMITS DEMAND (PERSONAL INJURY — GENERIC)**:
A time-limited, unconditional offer to settle the plaintiff's personal-injury claim within the available liability-policy limits, directed to the defendant's liability carrier and tendered with the structural elements that — under the third-party bad-faith doctrines of most U.S. states — establish the carrier's potential exposure to an excess-judgment / bad-faith claim if the demand is unreasonably rejected. Target length: 4–7 pages depending on injury complexity, damages itemization, and bad-faith framework.

**WHAT THIS TEMPLATE IS — AND WHAT IT IS NOT**:
This is the GENERIC / TRUNK policy-limits demand for state-agnostic personal-injury claims. It is NOT a Texas Stowers demand — for that, use `policy_limits_demand_pi_tx`, which lays out the four-element Stowers framework and Tex. Ins. Code § 541 / § 542 / § 542A predicates. It is NOT a Massachusetts c. 93A / 176D demand — for that, use `demand_letter_pi_ma` (current Wave 0 template). It is NOT a Georgia Holt § 9-11-67.1 statutorily-codified time-limited demand — that is Wave 2. The generic template uses Crisci / Egan-family framing and defers state-specific bad-faith doctrine to JURISDICTION CONTEXT, with placeholder citations.

**DYNAMIC BEHAVIOR — READ documentOptions**:
- `subPractice`: `mva` | `premises` | `product_liability` | `workers_comp_third_party` | (omit for general PI)
- `policyTypes`: array — `auto_liability`, `umbrella`, `commercial_liability`, `homeowners`, `professional_liability` — frames the carriers and limits at issue
- `responseDeadlineDays`: numeric (default 30) — drives the time-limited acceptance window
- `damagesCategories`: array — same set as `demand_letter_pi`
- `liabilityClarity`: `clear_and_uncontested` (default — strongest bad-faith setup) | `clear_with_minor_dispute` | `disputed` (weakest setup; reconsider whether a policy-limits demand is appropriate)
- `requestPolicyLimitsDisclosure`: boolean (default true)
- `includeRoyalGlobeCaveat`: boolean (default true) — note that direct first-party action by the third-party plaintiff against the defendant's carrier is barred in most states absent assignment / judgment / state-specific exception
- `includeBadFaithRoadmap`: boolean (default true) — explicitly outline the bad-faith framework (NAIC UCSPA + Crisci / Egan / state-specific cite via JURISDICTION CONTEXT)
- `acceptanceMechanics`: `cashier_check_to_firm_within_window` (default) | `release_and_check_simultaneous_exchange` | `state_specific_bad_faith_safe_harbor` (defer to JURISDICTION CONTEXT)

**STRUCTURE** (follow line by line):

1. Date line.
2. VIA line (bold) — `**VIA Certified Mail — Return Receipt Requested AND Email**` (default; certified-mail receipt is the single most important documentation item for any later bad-faith claim).
3. Recipient block — defendant carrier; claims department; address; adjuster name + ID if known; claims-supervisor copy as a separate addressee where appropriate.
4. Re: block (multi-line, aligned labels):
   Re:    Our Client:           [client name]
          Your Insured:         [defendant name]
          Claim Number:         [claim number]
          Date of Loss:         [date of incident]
          Subject:              **POLICY LIMITS SETTLEMENT DEMAND — TIME LIMITED**
5. Greeting — `Dear [Adjuster name]:` if known, else `Dear Claims Manager:`.

**BODY SECTIONS** (in order):

### I. Introduction and Notice of Demand
One paragraph: this letter is a formal time-limited policy-limits settlement demand on behalf of [client name] arising from the [date of incident] loss. The demand is unconditional: upon timely acceptance and tender of the available limits within the period stated below, plaintiff will execute a release of the named insured for all claims arising from this incident, subject to standard release language (no admission of liability; no broader release of unrelated claims; no release of claims against non-insured tortfeasors; no release of any UM/UIM claim against plaintiff's own carrier). State explicitly: 'This is a fully-formed offer of settlement; no further negotiation is required for acceptance.'

### II. Liability — Clarity and Foundation
Two to three paragraphs setting out the liability foundation. Adapt by `subPractice` and `liabilityClarity`:
- `mva`: cite the at-fault driver's traffic-law violation (using JURISDICTION CONTEXT for the state vehicle-code citation; otherwise `[STATE VEHICLE CODE PROVISION]`); identify point of impact, mechanism, and police-report number; quote any admission against interest or citation issued.
- `premises`: identify the dangerous condition, the owner/occupant's actual or constructive notice, and the duty owed under premises-liability law; quote any incident-report admission.
- `product_liability`: identify the product, defect category (design / manufacturing / warning), recall history if any; reference Restatement (Second) of Torts § 402A.
- General: Restatement (Second) of Torts § 282 negligence framing.
When `liabilityClarity=clear_and_uncontested`, state: 'Liability is clear. There is no good-faith basis for the carrier to dispute liability.' When `liabilityClarity=clear_with_minor_dispute`, identify and dispose of any contested-liability theory the carrier has previously raised. When `liabilityClarity=disputed`, reconsider whether a policy-limits demand is the right instrument — this template strongly disfavors policy-limits demands in genuinely-disputed-liability matters, and the user should weigh whether `demand_letter_pi` is more appropriate.

### III. Damages — Foundation Substantially Exceeding Limits
Damages section (subordinate to but more rigorous than `demand_letter_pi` Section III). Emit one sub-paragraph per `damagesCategories` entry:
- **Medical Expenses (incurred)**: itemize provider, dates of service, total billed.
- **Future Medical Expenses**: cite the treating-physician narrative report and any retained life-care planner / economist report supporting the projection.
- **Lost Wages (incurred)**: documented hours/days lost, source.
- **Future Earning Capacity**: vocational/economist projection.
- **Pain, Suffering, Loss of Enjoyment of Life**: 1–2 narrative paragraphs personalizing impact; cite Restatement (Second) of Torts § 905.
- **Loss of Consortium**: per-claimant breakdown.
- **Property Damage**: estimate or total-loss valuation.
Close the section with `**TOTAL DAMAGES: $______**` (bold) AND a comparative line: '**Total damages substantially exceed the available policy limits, making payment of limits the only good-faith resolution.**' Where the damages-to-limits ratio is high (3× or more), state it explicitly — that ratio is a key factual predicate for excess-judgment exposure analysis.

### IV. Policy Limits Disclosure Request [include only when requestPolicyLimitsDisclosure=true]
One paragraph: in connection with this demand, the carrier is requested to confirm in writing all available coverage — primary, excess, umbrella — covering the insured for this loss, including limits, declarations pages, and reservation-of-rights letters issued. Cite the state policy-limits disclosure statute via JURISDICTION CONTEXT (else `[STATE POLICY LIMITS DISCLOSURE STATUTE]`). Failure to disclose available coverage is itself probative of bad faith under most state UCSPA-derived statutes.

### V. The Demand
A bold, separately-displayed demand:
```
**DEMAND: $[available policy limits], constituting tender of the limits of all available coverage applicable to the [date of incident] loss.**
**ACCEPTANCE WINDOW: [responseDeadlineDays] days from the receipt of this letter.**
```
State explicitly: 'Acceptance of this demand requires (a) written confirmation from the carrier within the acceptance window, and (b) tender of the limits within thirty (30) days of acceptance, by cashier's check or wire payable to the firm's IOLTA / trust account, in exchange for an executed release. No counteroffer, partial offer, conditional tender, request for further information, or solicitation of additional documentation will constitute a timely response. Silence beyond the acceptance window will be treated as a rejection.'

### VI. Bad-Faith Framework [include only when includeBadFaithRoadmap=true]
One paragraph: outline the third-party bad-faith doctrine without overstating jurisdiction-specific elements. State:
- The carrier owes its insured a duty of good faith and fair dealing in the handling of third-party claims, including the duty to settle within policy limits when liability is reasonably clear and damages substantially exceed limits, citing **NAIC Unfair Claims Settlement Practices Act (Model)** and **Crisci v. Security Insurance Co., 66 Cal. 2d 425, 426 P.2d 173 (1967)** (Crisci-family framework) — defer state-specific cite to JURISDICTION CONTEXT (e.g., Egan, Royal Globe historical, state UCSPA codification).
- An unreasonable rejection of a within-limits offer that exposes the insured to an excess judgment may constitute bad faith and may give rise to:
  - The insured's assignment of the bad-faith claim to plaintiff,
  - A subsequent suit by plaintiff (as assignee) or by the insured directly against the carrier,
  - Recovery of the entire excess judgment, prejudgment interest, attorneys' fees, and (in some jurisdictions) punitive / multiplied damages.
- This letter is documented as primary evidence of the demand for purposes of any later excess-judgment analysis. Certified-mail receipt and email tracking are preserved.

### VII. Royal-Globe Caveat [include only when includeRoyalGlobeCaveat=true]
One paragraph: confirm understanding that — in most jurisdictions — the third-party plaintiff cannot sue the defendant's carrier directly for bad faith without (a) a judgment in excess of limits, (b) an assignment from the insured, or (c) a state-specific direct-action statute. This letter is therefore directed to the carrier in its capacity as agent for the insured, and is intended to crystallize the insured's potential excess-judgment exposure for the carrier's good-faith evaluation.

### VIII. Response Deadline and Reservation of Rights
One paragraph: please respond in writing within `responseDeadlineDays` days. Reserve all rights — additional damages, statutory remedies, litigation, and post-judgment proceedings to enforce excess judgments.

### IX. Closing
One sentence inviting the adjuster to call to discuss; provide direct line and email. Enclosures list — emit per `attachmentsList` (medical records, bills, wage documentation, photographs, police report, treating-physician narrative report, life-care plan, economist report).

Closing: `Very truly yours,` (suppress when stationery signature block is applied).

**ENCLOSURES BLOCK** (below signature, when `attachmentsList` is non-empty):
Enclosures: [each item, mapped to a human-readable label].

**CONSTRAINTS**:
- Do NOT cite a specific state bad-faith statute unless JURISDICTION CONTEXT supplies it. Use `[STATE UCSPA STATUTE]` placeholders. Generic Crisci / Egan reference is permitted as the federal-common-law-style framing.
- Do NOT include the four-element Stowers framework — that is `policy_limits_demand_pi_tx`.
- Do NOT include the c. 93A demand-letter / 30-day response window — that is `demand_letter_pi_ma`.
- Do NOT condition the demand on cooperation, additional documentation, or extraneous performance — conditions invalidate the bad-faith setup in many jurisdictions.
- Do NOT release claims against non-insured tortfeasors, plaintiff's own UM/UIM carrier, or any party not in privity with the demand. The release must be carefully scoped.
- Do NOT shorten the response deadline below twenty-one (21) days without specific case-circumstance support — short windows generate insurer 'unreasonable demand' defenses and may insulate the carrier from bad-faith exposure.
- For any disabled `damagesCategories` entry, suppress that bullet entirely.
- If firm stationery is applied, SUPPRESS the `Very truly yours,` closing and signature block.
- Total body text: 1,800–3,500 words depending on injury complexity. Target page count: 4–7 pages.
hints: Generic / state-agnostic third-party policy-limits demand — TRUNK template. State-specific siblings: policy_limits_demand_pi_tx (Stowers), demand_letter_pi_ma (c. 93A/176D). Cite NAIC UCSPA model and Crisci v. Security Insurance Co. (Cal. 1967) for bad-faith framework framing — defer state UCSPA codification to JURISDICTION CONTEXT. Demand is UNCONDITIONAL and TIME-LIMITED — default 30-day window. Include policy-limits disclosure request, bad-faith roadmap, Royal-Globe caveat. Liability must be clear-and-uncontested or the policy-limits demand is the wrong instrument. Reservation: damages must SUBSTANTIALLY EXCEED limits to make the demand a meaningful bad-faith setup. 4–7 pages.

verificationSources.citation: NAIC Unfair Claims Settlement Practices Act (model)
Crisci v. Security Insurance Co., 66 Cal. 2d 425, 426 P.2d 173 (1967)
Egan v. Mutual of Omaha Insurance Co., 24 Cal. 3d 809, 598 P.2d 452 (1979)
Restatement (Second) of Torts § 905 (compensatory damages for non-pecuniary loss)

knownLimitations: Generic — does NOT include state-specific bad-faith statutes or doctrines. Use state-specific siblings: policy_limits_demand_pi_tx (Stowers framework), demand_letter_pi_ma (c. 93A/176D), or future Wave 2 templates for FL § 624.155, GA § 9-11-67.1 Holt, IL § 155, NY § 2601.

Third-party bad-faith doctrine varies widely — some states recognize a duty to initiate settlement (Communale-style), others limit duty to responding to a within-limits demand. Some states have abrogated direct third-party bad-faith claims absent assignment (post-Royal-Globe).

Direct first-party action by the third-party plaintiff against the defendant's carrier is barred in most states absent (a) excess judgment, (b) assignment, or (c) state-specific direct-action statute. The Royal-Globe caveat addresses this.

Policy-limits demands are most effective where liability is clear AND damages substantially exceed limits. In genuinely-disputed-liability cases, a policy-limits demand may not constitute a meaningful bad-faith setup and may be the wrong instrument — consider demand_letter_pi instead.
Response deadlines below 21 days generate insurer 'unreasonable demand' defenses in many jurisdictions and may insulate the carrier from bad-faith exposure.
Acceptance mechanics (release-and-check exchange, IOLTA tender, structured settlement) vary by state and policy. Verify state-specific safe-harbor procedures before final send.
Coverage stacking, exhaustion order (primary / umbrella / excess), and other-insurance clauses materially affect the available limits — verify the coverage map before the demand.

Reservation-of-rights and declaratory-judgment posture by the carrier may affect the bad-faith analysis. If the carrier has reserved rights, that fact and the response strategy require attorney review.
